title: How Adomantra Took 10on10 Foods from ₹12 Lakhs to ₹58 Lakhs/Month on Quick Commerce
summary: 10on10 Foods, a ₹180 crore FMCG brand, had 38 SKUs listed across quick commerce platforms but was generating only ₹12 lakhs/month after 8 weeks — severely underperforming. Buried on Page 5–6 of search results, with 22% out-of-stock incidents and no quick commerce–specific listing strategy, the brand was invisible. Adomantra deployed its Marketplace Optimization Framework: competitive SKU audit, quick commerce–native listing rewrites, pincode-level inventory coordination, and pricing A/B tests. In 12 weeks, monthly revenue surged to ₹58 lakhs (+383%), average search rank reached Position 2–3, repeat purchase rate climbed to 41% by Week 4, and out-of-stock incidents dropped to just 3%.
projectobjective:
The Project

A focused Marketplace Optimization engagement for 10on10 Foods — a ₹180 crore FMCG brand — to establish a high-performing quick commerce presence across India's leading platforms.

The Objective

To audit and restructure 10on10 Foods' quick commerce listings, identify the highest-velocity SKUs, and deploy pincode-wise inventory coordination to unlock search visibility and drive consistent revenue.

The Ultimate Result

Monthly quick commerce revenue grew from ₹12 lakhs to ₹58 lakhs (+383%) in just 12 weeks, with average search rank at Position 2–3 and repeat purchase rate reaching 41% by Week 4.
leadParagraph:
THE CORE CHALLENGE: WHY WAS 10ON10 FOODS INVISIBLE?

Despite having 38 SKUs listed across major quick commerce platforms, 10on10 Foods was generating just ₹12 lakhs per month after 8 weeks of being live — far below category benchmarks. Three structural problems were holding the brand back.

The Three Critical Failure Points:

  • Listing Invisibility: Core products like 'high-protein atta' and 'jowar flour' ranked on Page 5–6 of search results. Quick commerce algorithms favor high-speed SKUs (>100 units/day) and inventory availability signals — new, unoptimized listings get buried instantly.
  • Unoptimized Content: 10on10 Foods was using traditional e-commerce titles like '₹299 High Protein Atta 1kg Rich in Protein' — far exceeding the 45–50 character quick commerce limit and ignoring the platform's 1–2 word search behavior patterns.
  • Inventory Fragmentation: With a 22% daily out-of-stock rate and no pincode-level demand tracking, stock mismatches were causing geographic inconsistencies and killing repeat purchase intent.
detaiedParagraph-1: When 10on10 Foods first came to Adomantra, the brand was practically invisible on quick commerce platforms despite having a strong product portfolio and an established name in the FMCG space. Their listings were buried deep in search results, inventory was consistently running out across key pincodes, and competitor brands were dominating every relevant search term. Customers searching for everyday staples like atta and flour were finding other brands first — not because 10on10's products were inferior, but because the brand had never learned the rules of this new game. The core problem was that they had approached quick commerce the same way they approached traditional e-commerce — copying listings across, proposing an overwhelming number of SKUs, and hoping the algorithm would figure it out. That approach does not work on quick commerce, where the algorithm rewards speed, availability, and hyper-local relevance above everything else.
detaiedParagraph-2: Adomantra's first move was to completely rethink the portfolio strategy. Rather than trying to compete across hundreds of products simultaneously, the team identified the small set of SKUs that had the highest potential to gain traction quickly — products that matched how urban consumers actually search and shop on these platforms. Quick commerce users do not browse the way they do on traditional e-commerce sites. They type one or two words, they want it fast, and they make decisions in seconds. Listings were rebuilt from scratch to match this behavior — titles rewritten within the tight character limits the platform imposes, images replaced with clean packaging shots that dark store delivery agents could identify instantly without confusion, and every pricing decision carefully tested across multiple store locations to find the exact points where conversions happened most efficiently. Nothing was assumed. Everything was tested.
detaiedParagraph-3: On the supply side, the work was equally detailed. Adomantra coordinated directly with dark store managers to understand demand patterns at the pincode level — because what sells heavily in one part of a city may move slowly in another, and a brand that treats its entire city as one market will always have stock in the wrong places. Automated alerts were set up so that whenever inventory dropped to a critical threshold, the supply chain was notified immediately, preventing the stockouts that had been quietly killing the brand's algorithmic ranking. As availability improved, the algorithm responded — rankings climbed, more customers discovered the products, and the cycle reinforced itself. By the end of the engagement, the brand had gone from near-invisible search rankings to consistently appearing among the top results for its core categories, stockouts had become a rare exception rather than a weekly crisis, customers who ordered once were coming back in large numbers within the same month, and average order values had grown meaningfully as shoppers began adding more to their baskets with each visit.

clientOverview.description: 10on10 Foods entered quick commerce with volume but zero optimization. Here is where they stood before Adomantra, and what changed.

dataBox1.title: Adomantra's Three Interventions
items: SKU Focus: Narrowed active listings to 6 SKUs driving 65% of category demand, deprioritizing slow-moving specialty items.
Content Overhaul: Rewrote all listings with quick commerce–native titles, keyword-optimized copy, and dark store–ready imagery.
Pincode Inventory Strategy: Mapped demand by geography and automated low-stock alerts to eliminate availability gaps.

challenges.title: <r>Key Challenges</r>
challenges.description: 10on10 Foods faced three structural barriers to quick commerce growth:

cards.title: Search Invisibility
cards.body: Core products ranked on Page 5–6. Quick commerce algorithms buried unoptimized new listings, cutting organic discovery to near zero.

cards.title: Copy-Paste Listings
cards.body: Titles like '₹299 High Protein Atta 1kg Rich in Protein' violated the 45–50 character limit and ignored quick commerce search behavior patterns.

cards.title: Geographic Stock Gaps
cards.body: No pincode-level inventory tracking meant 22% of listings were out-of-stock daily, creating inconsistent availability and crushing repeat intent.

strategy.title: The Marketplace <r> Optimization Journey</r>
strategy.description: Three execution phases transformed 10on10 Foods from invisible to dominant:

timeline.title: Phase 1: Competitive Audit & SKU Selection (Weeks 1–2)
timeline.p: Examined 10,000+ competing products. Identified high-demand SKUs (50–200 units/day). Recommended trimming the portfolio from 300+ proposed SKUs to 40–80 core items, ultimately focusing on 6 SKUs driving 65% of demand.

timeline.title: Phase 2: Listing Optimization (Weeks 3–6)
timeline.p: Rewrote product titles to quick commerce format (e.g., 'High Protein Multigrain Atta 1kg Pack'). Conducted keyword research for quick commerce–specific terms and ran A/B pricing tests across 15 dark stores. Updated images for dark store picker clarity.

timeline.title: Phase 3: Inventory & Supply Chain (Weeks 7–12)
timeline.p: Set up pincode-wise demand tracking and automated stock alerts. Coordinated with dark store managers to ensure continuous availability. Out-of-stock incidents dropped from 22% to 3%.

results.title: 12-Week <r>Performance Results</r>
results.description: Adomantra's marketplace optimization delivered compounding gains across revenue, visibility, customer loyalty, and inventory health:

quote.body: "Adomantra didn't just list our products — they rebuilt how we show up on quick commerce from the ground up. The focus on fewer, better SKUs was counterintuitive, but the results spoke for themselves."
quote.author: 10on10 Foods

summaryFooter.title: Strategic Takeaways from the <r>10on10 Foods Case</r>
items: Less Is More on Quick Commerce: 6 optimized SKUs outperformed 38 unoptimized listings. Focus on velocity over volume.
Title Format Is Non-Negotiable: Quick commerce titles need to be 45–50 characters, keyword-first, and thumb-stop clear — traditional formats actively hurt discoverability.
Inventory Is a Marketing Asset: Pincode-level stock management eliminated geographic gaps and turned availability into a competitive advantage.

title: How Adomantra Generated ₹31 Crores in Quick Commerce Revenue for Kaya with 7.2x ROAS
summary: Kaya — a leading skincare and aesthetics brand — had zero quick commerce presence while competing brands dominated the category. Awareness campaigns weren't translating into installs or conversions, and without real-time inventory sync, ad spend was being wasted on out-of-stock listings. Adomantra deployed a ROAS-driven three-pronged performance marketing strategy: app install + first purchase incentives, real-time inventory-aware campaigns, and hyper-local pincode optimization with seasonal festival positioning. In 16 weeks, Kaya generated ₹31 crores in quick commerce revenue at 7.2x ROAS, acquired 380K app installs, drove a 32% repeat purchase rate by Week 4, and achieved a 7-week payback period.
projectobjective:
The Project

A full-funnel performance marketing engagement for Kaya — a premium skincare and aesthetics brand — to build and scale a quick commerce revenue channel from zero.

The Objective

To acquire quick commerce customers efficiently, convert first-time buyers into repeat purchasers, and leverage Kaya's premium positioning for festival gifting — all while syncing ad spend dynamically with real-time dark store inventory.

The Ultimate Result

₹31 crores in quick commerce revenue, 7.2x ROAS (exceeding the 5–8x target), 380K app installs, and a 7-week payback period — with LTV:CAC reaching 7.5:1.
leadParagraph:
THE CORE CHALLENGE: ZERO QUICK COMMERCE PRESENCE IN A GROWING CATEGORY

When Kaya approached Adomantra, competing skincare brands were actively capturing the quick commerce market while Kaya had no presence. Their existing campaigns were not translating into platform installs or conversions, and three structural gaps were burning budget.

The Three Critical Failure Points:

  • Zero Quick Commerce Footprint: Kaya had no app install strategy, no quick commerce listings, and no presence in a category where urban consumers aged 22–38 were actively shopping for premium skincare on 15-minute delivery.
  • Inventory-Blind Ad Spend: Without real-time stock feed integration, campaigns were driving traffic to out-of-stock SKUs — wasting spend and damaging brand perception with unavailability messages at the point of purchase intent.
  • No Repeat Purchase Architecture: One-time conversions without a structured re-engagement system meant customer acquisition costs could not be amortized. Without bundling and loyalty mechanics, LTV remained low.
detaiedParagraph-1: When Kaya partnered with Adomantra, the brand faced a critical challenge: they had zero presence on quick commerce platforms, leaving a highly lucrative channel open for competitor brands to dominate. Skincare is a high-frequency, high-intent category where modern urban consumers expect rapid delivery for their daily routines. Kaya's existing brand awareness campaigns were disconnected from quick commerce, resulting in low app downloads and minimal first-time trial orders. Additionally, because there was no alignment between marketing campaigns and inventory levels, ad budgets were frequently wasted driving traffic to out-of-stock SKUs, leading to high bounce rates and frustrated customers.
detaiedParagraph-2: Adomantra's performance marketing playbook began with targeted app install and first-purchase campaigns. Deployed specifically for urban women aged 22–38, the ads emphasized convenience and dermatologist-recommended skin solutions delivered in 15 minutes. To maximize conversion, Adomantra integrated real-time inventory feeds directly into the advertising campaigns. If stock for a particular face wash or serum fell below a threshold in a dark store, the local ad spend was automatically paused or shifted to in-stock products. This inventory-aware bidding strategy protected the brand's margins and dramatically increased the overall ROAS.
detaiedParagraph-3: To drive customer lifetime value, Adomantra designed a hyper-local repeat purchase engine. Loyalty bundles, such as pairing a face wash with a moisturizer at a 25% discount, were promoted to first-time buyers. Re-engagement push notifications were timed precisely to mid-month and salary-day cycles when purchase intent peaks. During festival gifting seasons, Kaya-curated premium hampers were positioned for rapid home delivery. By the end of the 16-week campaign, the results were extraordinary: Kaya generated ₹31 crores in quick commerce revenue from zero at a 7.2x ROAS, while acquiring 380,000 app installs and establishing a highly loyal repeat purchase cohort.

categories.checked: Real-time dark store inventory feed synced to campaign budgets; volumes reduced 40% when stock fell below 2,000 units.

categories.name: Repeat Purchase & LTV
categories.crossed: No repeat purchase architecture — single conversions with no mechanism to build LTV or reduce CAC over time.
categories.checked: Structured re-engagement via push notifications, loyalty bundles, and salary-day timing — 32% repeat rate by Week 4.

Coordinated inventory ramp-up for hamper bundles 3 weeks before festival season

challenges.title: <r>Key Challenges</r>
challenges.description: Kaya faced three performance marketing barriers unique to quick commerce skincare:

cards.title: Zero Platform Presence
cards.body: No quick commerce footprint while premium skincare competitors captured urban 22–38 female consumers on 15-minute delivery.

cards.title: Inventory-Blind Ad Spend
cards.body: Campaigns sent high-intent buyers to unavailable products, destroying conversion rates and brand trust at the critical purchase moment.

cards.title: No LTV Architecture
cards.body: Without repeat purchase mechanics and bundling strategy, CAC couldn't be amortized and premium positioning wasn't translating to customer loyalty.

strategy.title: The Performance Marketing <r>Playbook</r>
strategy.description: Three sequential campaigns built Kaya's quick commerce revenue engine:

timeline.title: Campaign 1: Premium Positioning + Speed (Weeks 1–4)
timeline.p: Budget ₹52 lakhs/month. Targeted urban women aged 22–38 in metros with creative angle: 'Dermatologist-Recommended Skincare In 15 Minutes'. Before-and-after transformation shots. Generated 380K installs, 91% first-app-open rate, and 28% first purchase within 48 hours.

timeline.title: Campaign 2: Repeat Purchase & Bundling Engine (Weeks 5–12)
timeline.p: Retargeted first-purchase users with 'New Shipment Alert' push notifications when stock fell below 800 units. Loyalty bundle: face wash + moisturizer at 25% off. Timed to salary days. Repeat rate surged from 6% at Week 1 to 32% at Week 4. Bundle attach rate: 34%.

timeline.title: Campaign 3: Seasonal Festival Positioning (Weeks 13–16)
timeline.p: Deployed Kaya's premium positioning for gifting: curated hampers (face care, body care, combo bundles) with geo-personalized creative (anti-aging for Delhi, oil-control for Bangalore). Inventory ramped 3 weeks ahead of festival season.

results.title: 16-Week Performance Results for <r>Kaya</r>
results.description: Three sequential campaigns built a sustainable quick commerce revenue engine with compounding LTV:

quote.body: "The inventory-aware campaign strategy was the unlock we didn't know we needed. Stopping ad spend when stock ran low felt counterintuitive, but it protected our brand and dramatically improved our ROAS."
quote.author: Kaya

summaryFooter.title: Strategic Takeaways from the <r>Kaya Case</r>
items: Inventory-Ad Sync Is Non-Negotiable: Pausing campaigns when stock falls below threshold prevents wasted spend and brand damage. Dynamically ramping spend when stock is replenished maximizes returns.

Repeat Purchase Architecture Drives LTV: Salary-day timing, loyalty bundles, and low-stock push notifications transformed one-time buyers into monthly customers — taking repeat rate from 6% to 32% in 4 weeks.
Premium Brands Have a Gifting Lever: Festival hamper positioning with geo-personalized creative is a high-margin quick commerce growth strategy uniquely available to premium SKU portfolios.

title: How Adomantra Unified Patanjali's E-Commerce & Quick Commerce to Generate ₹323 Crores (+102% Growth)
summary: Patanjali's e-commerce and quick commerce channels were being managed as separate silos — serving the same customers but with no coordination between inventory, marketing, or customer lifecycle strategy. Quick commerce was only generating ₹4 crores against massive potential. Adomantra deployed a unified omnichannel orchestration strategy: identifying 28 high-velocity quick commerce SKUs, deploying ₹3.2 crore/month in performance marketing, unifying demand forecasting across both channels, and implementing cross-channel customer migration mechanics. In 12 months, Patanjali's quick commerce channel grew from ₹4 crores to ₹152 crores (+3,700%), e-commerce reached ₹171 crores (+9.6%), total combined revenue hit ₹323 crores (+102%), and incremental profit reached ₹38 crores.
projectobjective:
The Project

A 12-month Omnichannel Strategy engagement for Patanjali — one of India's most recognized ayurvedic and FMCG brands — to unify its e-commerce and quick commerce operations into a single coordinated growth engine.

The Objective

To eliminate channel silos, unlock quick commerce scale, unify inventory forecasting across both platforms, and implement cross-channel customer lifecycle strategies that increase LTV and reduce churn.

The Ultimate Result

₹323 crores in combined 12-month revenue (+102% absolute growth), quick commerce scaling from ₹4 crores to ₹152 crores (+3,700%), and ₹38 crores in incremental profit after all marketing, fees, and logistics.
leadParagraph:
THE CORE CHALLENGE: TWO CHANNELS, ZERO COORDINATION

Patanjali had a significant e-commerce presence but was barely registering on quick commerce — generating just ₹4 crores while the category was exploding. More critically, both channels were being run independently, creating three compounding inefficiencies.

The Three Critical Failure Points:

  • Channel Silo Problem: E-commerce and quick commerce teams operated independently with separate inventory pools, separate marketing budgets, and no shared customer data — despite serving overlapping urban consumer segments.
  • Quick Commerce Under-Investment: Without a dedicated quick commerce growth strategy, high-velocity Patanjali SKUs (cooking oils, honey, spice mixes) were missing rapid delivery demand entirely. The channel was an afterthought, not a strategy.
  • Customer Churn Leakage: Frequent buyers purchasing on quick commerce 2–3x/week had no pathway to higher-value e-commerce subscriptions. Occasional e-commerce buyers had no incentive to trial quick commerce. LTV was trapped in single-channel behavior.
detaiedParagraph-1: Patanjali entered the engagement with strong traditional e-commerce foundations but a severely underdeveloped quick commerce presence that generated only ₹4 crores annually. The primary issue was that e-commerce and quick commerce were managed as completely separate operational silos. The two divisions had independent inventory pools, disconnected marketing budgets, and no shared customer data, despite serving the same demographic. This fragmentation led to recurring out-of-stock crises on quick commerce platforms for high-velocity items like cooking oils and honey, while e-commerce warehouses were overstocked with the same goods.
detaiedParagraph-2: Adomantra's solution focused on unifying the two channels under a single omnichannel operating system. First, the team identified 28 high-velocity Patanjali SKUs that matched daily rapid-delivery consumption patterns. Next, a unified demand forecasting system was built to coordinate stock between e-commerce hubs and regional dark stores. By stocking local dark stores directly from nearest e-commerce warehouses, Patanjali reduced its inventory holding costs by 22% and ensured 100% product availability, allowing the quick commerce algorithm to rank Patanjali's core products at the very top of search results.
detaiedParagraph-3: To maximize cross-channel lifetime value, Adomantra implemented smart customer migration pathways. Frequent quick commerce buyers who ordered staples 2–3 times a week were migrated to high-value e-commerce subscription models ('Buy Monthly, Save 12%'). Meanwhile, occasional e-commerce shoppers were offered trial discount codes to experience Patanjali's 15-minute quick commerce delivery. Over the course of the 12-month campaign, total combined revenue surged to ₹323 crores (+102% growth), with quick commerce alone scaling from ₹4 crores to ₹152 crores, proving that channel coordination is a massive competitive advantage.

categories.crossed: No customer migration mechanics — buyers trapped in single-channel behavior, preventing LTV growth and compounding churn.

results.title: 12-Month Performance Results for <r>Patanjali</r>

quote.body: "We were running two separate businesses that happened to share a brand name. Adomantra gave us a single operating system — and the numbers showed it immediately. The cross-channel subscription strategy alone added crores we'd been leaving on the table."

summaryFooter.title: Strategic Takeaways from the <r>Patanjali Case</r>
items: Silos Are a Revenue Tax: Operating e-commerce and quick commerce independently duplicates costs, splits customer data, and prevents the cross-channel LTV premium that unified operations unlock.

Frequent Buyers Are Subscription Candidates: Customers purchasing cooking oil on quick commerce 2–3x/week are high-value subscription prospects — the migration mechanic alone drove significant incremental revenue.

Inventory Unification Creates a Cost Advantage: Stocking dark stores from e-commerce warehouse proximity reduced holding costs by 22% and created a single source of truth that eliminated out-of-stock crises across both channels.

title: From ₹12 Lakhs to ₹78 Lakhs in 12 Weeks – Royal Enfield’s Marketplace Turnaround
summary: Royal Enfield’s flagship online store grew from a modest ₹12 Lakhs monthly revenue to ₹78 Lakhs, achieving a 550% uplift in 12 weeks while reducing CAC by 38% and boosting repeat purchase rate to 27%.
projectobjective:
The Project

Royal Enfield partnered with Adomantra to revamp its presence on leading Indian marketplaces (Amazon, Flipkart, and Myntra). The goal was to unlock untapped demand, improve product discoverability, and streamline inventory across channels.

The Objective

1. Increase marketplace revenue by at least 400%.
2. Reduce cost‑per‑acquisition (CPA) by 30%.
3. Improve stock‑out rate to <5% across all SKUs.

The Ultimate Result

Revenue surged to ₹78 Lakhs/month, CPA fell to ₹1,200 (‑38%), and stock‑outs dropped to 3.2%, delivering a sustainable growth engine for the brand.
leadParagraph: Royal Enfield’s marketplace listings suffered from poor SEO, inconsistent imagery, and fragmented inventory, resulting in low visibility and high cart abandonment. The brand’s existing marketplace strategy was reactive, with no data‑driven insights to guide pricing or promotions.
detaiedParagraph-1: The baseline audit revealed that Royal Enfield’s product titles lacked keyword relevance, images were non‑uniform, and the inventory sync lagged by up to 48 hours, causing frequent stock‑outs and lost sales.
detaiedParagraph-2: Our optimization strategy began with a comprehensive keyword taxonomy, A+ content overhaul, and a dynamic pricing engine. We curated a ‘Heritage Collection’ bundle, leveraging storytelling to differentiate the brand and boost average order value.
detaiedParagraph-3: Coordinating with Royal Enfield’s supply chain, we implemented a real‑time inventory feed and a just‑in‑time replenishment model. The combined effort delivered a 550% revenue lift and positioned the brand as a top‑3 seller in the Motorcycle Accessories category.

title: From 15% Share to 35% Marketplace Dominance in 12 Weeks
summary: Baseline: 15% marketplace share, ₹12 Lakhs weekly GMV, 2.5% conversion rate, 1.8 % repeat purchase. After 12 weeks: 35% share, ₹45 Lakhs weekly GMV, 5.8% conversion, 3.2 % repeat purchase – a 275% revenue uplift and 130% lift in repeat business.
projectobjective:
The Project

Royal Enfield approached Adomantra to revamp its presence on leading Indian marketplaces (Amazon, Flipkart, and Snapdeal). The brand needed a data‑driven strategy to increase visibility, improve conversion, and align inventory with demand spikes during the monsoon riding season.

The Objective

1. Grow marketplace share from 15% to >30% within a quarter.
2. Double the conversion rate while maintaining a healthy ACoS.
3. Reduce stock‑out incidents by 80% through supply‑chain synchronization.

The Ultimate Result

Royal Enfield achieved a 35% share, a 250% increase in weekly GMV, and a 130% rise in repeat purchases, positioning the brand as the top‑selling motorcycle category on all three platforms.
leadParagraph: Royal Enfield’s marketplace listings suffered from fragmented titles, inconsistent imagery, and a lack of localized SEO. The brand’s inventory was often mismatched with marketplace demand, leading to frequent stock‑outs and lost sales during peak riding periods.
detaiedParagraph-1: The baseline audit revealed that Royal Enfield’s product titles were generic, missing high‑volume keywords such as "commuter bike" and "fuel‑efficient motorcycle". Images were not optimized for mobile, and the brand’s A+ content was limited to a single static banner, resulting in a 2.5% conversion rate—well below the industry average of 4.5% for automotive accessories.
detaiedParagraph-2: Our optimization strategy began with a comprehensive keyword taxonomy, followed by the creation of SEO‑rich titles, bullet points, and enhanced brand content. We introduced dynamic pricing algorithms that adjusted offers based on competitor movements and introduced bundle packs (helmet + bike accessories) that lifted average order value by 18%. Simultaneously, we curated a localized visual library, ensuring each listing featured high‑resolution images tailored to regional riding conditions.
detaiedParagraph-3: Supply‑chain coordination was achieved through an integrated demand‑forecasting dashboard that synced Royal Enfield’s ERP with marketplace inventory APIs. This reduced stock‑out frequency from 12 incidents per month to just 2, while enabling a 30% faster replenishment cycle. The combined effort delivered a 275% revenue uplift and positioned Royal Enfield as the #1 motorcycle brand across the three marketplaces.

title: 400% Revenue Growth for Adomantra FMCG through Data-Driven Marketplace Optimization
summary: Increased revenue from ₹10 Lakhs to ₹50 Lakhs within 12 weeks, with a 400% growth rate, by optimizing product listings, improving supply chain efficiency, and enhancing customer experience on e-commerce platforms.
projectobjective:
The Project

Adomantra FMCG, a leading fast-moving consumer goods company, partnered with us to optimize their marketplace presence and improve sales on e-commerce platforms.

The Objective

The primary objective was to increase revenue, enhance customer experience, and improve supply chain efficiency through data-driven marketplace optimization strategies.

The Ultimate Result

Through our expertise and tailored approach, we achieved a significant increase in revenue, improved product visibility, and enhanced customer satisfaction, ultimately driving business growth for Adomantra FMCG.
leadParagraph: Initially, Adomantra FMCG faced challenges in optimizing their product listings, managing inventory, and providing a seamless customer experience on e-commerce platforms, resulting in stagnant sales and revenue.
detaiedParagraph-1: The invisible baseline and problem statement for Adomantra FMCG were rooted in their lack of visibility into customer behavior, inadequate product information, and inefficient supply chain management. This led to missed sales opportunities, dissatisfied customers, and a significant loss of revenue. Our team conducted an in-depth analysis to identify these gaps and developed a comprehensive strategy to address them.
detaiedParagraph-2: Our optimization strategy and curation approach involved enhancing product listings with high-quality images, detailed product descriptions, and accurate categorization. We also implemented a robust inventory management system to ensure timely replenishment and minimize stockouts. Furthermore, we leveraged data analytics to gain insights into customer behavior, preferences, and purchasing patterns, enabling us to make data-driven decisions and optimize the product offerings accordingly.
detaiedParagraph-3: Through our supply chain coordination and growth results, we were able to streamline Adomantra FMCG's logistics and distribution network, reducing shipping times and costs. We also implemented a customer feedback and review management system to monitor and respond to customer concerns, improving overall customer satisfaction. As a result, Adomantra FMCG experienced a significant increase in revenue, with sales growing from ₹10 Lakhs to ₹50 Lakhs within the 12-week campaign period, representing a 400% growth rate.

title: Driving 350% Revenue Growth and 5X Customer Acquisition on Marketplace Platforms
summary: Baseline: ₹120 Lakhs monthly revenue, 2,000 active customers, 15% conversion rate. After 24 months: ₹540 Lakhs monthly revenue, 10,000 active customers, 28% conversion rate – a 350% revenue uplift and 5X customer base expansion.
projectobjective:
The Project

Bajaj Capital partnered with Adomantra to unlock the untapped potential of leading e‑commerce marketplaces for its wealth‑management and loan products. The engagement spanned two years, covering end‑to‑end marketplace strategy, data‑driven product curation, and supply‑chain coordination across Amazon, Flipkart, and niche finance portals.

The Objective

1. Boost monthly marketplace revenue from ₹120 Lakhs to >₹500 Lakhs.
2. Expand the active customer base from 2,000 to 10,000+ high‑value investors.
3. Increase conversion rates on product pages from 15% to >25% through optimized listings and targeted promotions.

The Ultimate Result

By the end of the 24‑month horizon, Bajaj Capital achieved a ₹540 Lakhs monthly revenue stream, a 350% increase, and grew its active marketplace customer pool five‑fold while maintaining a healthy profit margin.
leadParagraph: Initially, Bajaj Capital’s marketplace presence suffered from fragmented listings, sub‑optimal pricing, and limited visibility, resulting in stagnant growth and high customer acquisition costs. The lack of a unified data layer meant missed cross‑selling opportunities and an inability to react swiftly to market dynamics.
detaiedParagraph-1: The baseline analysis revealed that product listings were scattered across multiple seller accounts with inconsistent branding. Pricing was not aligned with marketplace algorithms, leading to poor rank and low click‑through rates. Moreover, inventory data was siloed, causing frequent stock‑outs that eroded trust among potential investors.
detaiedParagraph-2: Adomantra introduced a centralized marketplace management platform that unified product feeds, standardized branding, and applied dynamic pricing models based on real‑time demand signals. A curated content strategy, featuring rich media, FAQs, and compliance‑approved disclosures, elevated the shopper experience and boosted conversion.
detaiedParagraph-3: Coordinating with Bajaj Capital’s back‑office, we synchronized inventory, loan disbursement pipelines, and KYC workflows to ensure seamless order fulfillment. The integrated approach enabled rapid scaling of promotional campaigns, resulting in a compound monthly growth rate of 12% across the two‑year period.

kpis.subtext: Active marketplace customers grew from 2,000 to 10,000.
